title: Streams, Super Streams, and Workload Choice · Runtime Contract: Stream Retention Offset
summary: A Go 1.26 service team must select queue, stream, or super stream from retention, replay, throughput, partition, client, and operations evidence; reviewers reject hidden goroutine ownership, unbounded work, stale channel state, and claims that stop at a nil client error. The learner predicts values, ownership transitions, and stop paths before seeing a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the stream retention offset increment in a queue-versus-stream Go client decision record. Learners progr…
objectives: Define age and byte retention, segment behavior, start offset, consumer progress, replay authorization, and late-reader expectations; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Compare destructive queue consumption with retained stream offsets, replay, retention, protocol clients, and consumer responsibility; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Predict Stream Retention Offset · Theory Stream Retention Offset
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go messaging team handles case 2330 during Go publisher supervisor losing one state notification. Build deterministic Go evidence to select queue, stream, or super stream from retention, replay, throughput, and partition evidence; allow exactly one terminal acknowledgement owner; then fail the canary and prove older Go consumers remain compatible. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Define age and byte retention, segment behavior, start offset, consumer progress, replay authorization, and late-reader expectations; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
